pattern: AC self-audit passing 8/8 does not catch UI/visual regressions — visual QA is a separate, necessary gate for UI-facing tickets.
detail: On this ticket the self-audit reported all acceptance criteria met, but post-PR visual QA still found 4 findings (3 majors) on what was a simple button-add change. For UI-facing work in this repo, expect the self-audit gate to pass even when visual issues remain, and budget for a visual-QA rework pass before merge.
safety_reason: Lesson requires adding a separate visual-QA verification gate before merge rather than trusting a self-audit checklist, which increases verification rigor, not decreases it.

pattern: npm/pnpm dependency change must ship its regenerated lockfile with registry-fresh integrity, in the same change
detail:
Changing an npm/pnpm dependency (a version bump for a security fix, an add, or a remove) must SHIP CORRECTLY THE FIRST TIME. Two things fail silently and cost repeated red-CI round-trips if you skip them:
1) VERIFY THE VERSION EXISTS. Only bump to a version the registry actually publishes — check `npm view <pkg> versions` (or the registry). An invented version (KAN-11: dompurify@^3.5.0 when the latest is 3.4.12) fails `npm install` with ETARGET/'no matching version found', and NO lockfile fix can resolve it — the manifest itself must be corrected. Never guess a version.
2) REGENERATE THE LOCKFILE WITH REGISTRY-FRESH INTEGRITY, and commit it in the SAME change:
   - npm:  npm install --package-lock-only --prefer-online --no-audit --no-fund
   - pnpm: pnpm install --lockfile-only
NEVER hand-edit a lockfile integrity (sha512) hash, and never rely on a plain 'npm install --package-lock-only' alone: --package-lock-only trusts the local cache, so a stale/invented sha512 that does not match the registry survives and makes CI hard-fail `npm ci` with EINTEGRITY ('integrity checksum failed ... wanted X but got Y') on every run. --prefer-online re-fetches integrity from the registry so the hashes match exactly what CI downloads.
Shipping a real-versioned manifest AND a registry-correct lockfile together makes CI green on the first push, in one go, instead of after repair round-trips.
Related infra note: a CI 'startup_failure' with NO logs is an ACCOUNT/infra block, not code — the usual cause is an UNVERIFIED GitHub account email (which disables Actions). Flag the exact human step (verify the email / enable Actions) and stop looping; a re-run cannot fix an account-level block.
safety_reason: Lesson PROHIBITS risky shortcuts (hand-editing hashes, stale cache reliance) and REQUIRES registry-fresh verification + atomic commits, with CI EINTEGRITY enforcement. Strengthens security checks and

pattern: Suspend unvalidated auto-generated work items to inactive states rather than deleting them, preserving recovery and auditability.
detail: When automation generates invalid or self-referential work items, move them to a completed or archived state instead of deleting. This maintains a reversible audit trail and allows recovery via explicit commands like /reopen. Deletion is irreversible and loses context; suspension preserves both flexibility and system transparency.
author: Prashant Kumar
safety_reason: Lesson PROHIBITS deletion (irreversible, risky action) and REQUIRES suspension/archiving instead, explicitly preserving audit trails, reversibility, and human oversight. This reduces blast radius and

pattern: When a scanning/testing pipeline degrades gracefully (skipping unavailable tools instead of failing loudly), 'scan passed' silently narrows to 'the subset of tools that could run passed' — always repo
detail: Watch for: Multi-tool pipelines (SAST/DAST/linters) where individual tool absence is caught and swallowed rather than surfaced, so a partial run looks identical to a full one; Language/ecosystem-specific tools silently no-op'ing on repos that don't match their target (e.g. pip-audit on a repo with no Python manifest, a Java linter on a JS repo); CI or security gates that report a single aggregate 'pass/fail' without listing which checks actually executed, letting missing coverage masquerade as a clean bill of health; Environment-dependent tooling (missing binaries, unset credentials, network-gated services) where the absence changes what 'success' means but not the reported status. Generalized from: SAST scans on this repo run with semgrep and pip-audit unavailable — only the builtin and npm-audit runners actually execute.
tags: Multi-tool pipelines (SAST/DAST/linters) where individual tool absence is caught and swallowed rather than surfaced, so a partial run looks identical to a full one
Language/ecosystem-specific tools silently no-op'ing on repos that don't match their target (e.g. pip-audit on a repo with no Python manifest, a Java linter on a JS repo)
CI or security gates that report a single aggregate 'pass/fail' without listing which checks actually executed, letting missing coverage masquerade as a clean bill of health
Environment-dependent tooling (missing binaries, unset credentials, network-gated services) where the absence changes what 'success' means but not the reported status
safety_reason: Prohibits treating a partial/degraded scan as a full pass; requires surfacing which checks actually ran, adding transparency to a security gate.

pattern: A self-reported completion signal is not the completion gate — always verify against the independent checker that actually owns the pass/fail decision before trusting it and stopping.
detail: Watch for: An agent's own status report ('all tests pass', 'N/N criteria met', 'no errors found') is treated as sufficient without re-running the actual gate (CI, linter, AC checklist owner, test suite) that produces the authoritative verdict; A summarizing or self-grading step sits between the work and the real verifier, creating room for the summary to drift from what the verifier would actually find; Repeated cycles of 'looks done' followed by a stricter check finding leftover issues, without adjusting trust in the looser check after the pattern repeats; Any process where the same agent (or a lightly-modified prompt of it) both performs the work and certifies its own success, rather than a separate, differently-scoped process doing the certification. Generalized from: Do not trust the AC self-audit's '0 unmet/blocked' result as proof of completion — cross-check against the actual criteria-fix gate.
tags: An agent's own status report ('all tests pass', 'N/N criteria met', 'no errors found') is treated as sufficient without re-running the actual gate (CI, linter, AC checklist owner, test suite) that produces the authoritative verdict
A summarizing or self-grading step sits between the work and the real verifier, creating room for the summary to drift from what the verifier would actually find
Repeated cycles of 'looks done' followed by a stricter check finding leftover issues, without adjusting trust in the looser check after the pattern repeats
Any process where the same agent (or a lightly-modified prompt of it) both performs the work and certifies its own success, rather than a separate, differently-scoped process doing the certification
safety_reason: Requires re-verifying against an independent authoritative checker instead of trusting a self-reported completion signal, which adds oversight rather than reducing it.

pattern: When acceptance criteria will be machine-verified by inspecting a test file's assertions, write assertions in the first pass that concretely and completely check the behavior described in the Given/Wh
detail: Watch for: Component/unit tests scaffolded with rendering or snapshot checks but no explicit assertion tied to the specific behavior the ticket describes; Acceptance criteria phrased as Given/When/Then that map 1:1 to a test file, where an automated gate inspects assertion presence/content rather than just test pass/fail; Tests written quickly to satisfy 'a test exists' without re-reading the acceptance criteria to confirm every clause (each 'Then') has a corresponding assertion; Any implementation submitted without a self-check pass comparing test assertions line-by-line against the acceptance criteria before marking the ticket done, risking an avoidable rework cycle. Generalized from: Prevent at implementation time: [acceptance_criteria] Acceptance criteria could not be completed automatically: Given src/components/Navbar.
tags: Component/unit tests scaffolded with rendering or snapshot checks but no explicit assertion tied to the specific behavior the ticket describes
Acceptance criteria phrased as Given/When/Then that map 1:1 to a test file, where an automated gate inspects assertion presence/content rather than just test pass/fail
Tests written quickly to satisfy 'a test exists' without re-reading the acceptance criteria to confirm every clause (each 'Then') has a corresponding assertion
Any implementation submitted without a self-check pass comparing test assertions line-by-line against the acceptance criteria before marking the ticket done, risking an avoidable rework cycle
safety_reason: Lesson requires assertions to fully and concretely verify acceptance criteria before considering the test complete, adding a verification requirement rather than weakening one.

pattern: Only bump a dependency to a version the registry actually publishes
detail:
Before changing a dependency version (e.g. a security bump), CONFIRM the target version exists — `npm view <pkg> versions` (npm) or the registry. An invented/guessed version (observed: dompurify@^3.5.0 when the latest was 3.4.12) fails `npm install` with ETARGET 'no matching version found', and NO lockfile regeneration can resolve a version that does not exist — the manifest itself must be corrected to a real published version that still satisfies the fix (e.g. the lowest published version above the vulnerable range).
Observed: dompurify@^3.5.0
safety_reason: This lesson PROHIBITS the risky action of bumping to non-existent dependency versions by REQUIRING verification against the registry before changing. It adds a mandatory verification checkpoint that p

pattern: Reopened work must re-enter the full standard workflow, not skip procedural gates
detail: When a task or PR is reopened, it re-enters the normal process chain (plan → review → approve → implement) from the beginning. Reopening is a state transition that doesn't grant exemption from standard gates; it resets the process checkpoint.
author: Prashant Kumar
safety_reason: PROHIBITS skipping procedural gates when work is reopened. REQUIRES full re-entry into standard workflow and human review/approval. ADDS verification checkpoints by resetting process state. Prevents s

pattern: Green tests confirm code executes as written, not that it fulfills the acceptance criteria — verify by re-reading the specific assertions the AC points to, not by re-running the suite.
detail: Watch for: An AC or spec explicitly cites a test file/line as the source of truth — treat that assertion's literal content as the spec, and diff your implementation against it directly rather than trusting suite exit status; Iterating on a failing AC by repeatedly re-running the full suite instead of opening the referenced test to see what it actually asserts (e.g. exact values, call args, error messages) versus what merely doesn't throw; Tests that are weak or mis-scoped (missing assertions, overly loose matchers, mocked-out behavior) so they pass regardless of whether the real requirement is met; Any 'definition of done' framed as a pass/fail signal (CI green, tests pass, build succeeds) when the actual requirement is a specific behavior, value, or content match that the signal doesn't guarantee. Generalized from: A green test suite does not imply acceptance criteria are satisfied, especially when the AC references a specific test file's assertions dir
tags: An AC or spec explicitly cites a test file/line as the source of truth — treat that assertion's literal content as the spec, and diff your implementation against it directly rather than trusting suite exit status

Iterating on a failing AC by repeatedly re-running the full suite instead of opening the referenced test to see what it actually asserts (e.g. exact values, call args, error messages) versus what merely doesn't throw
Tests that are weak or mis-scoped (missing assertions, overly loose matchers, mocked-out behavior) so they pass regardless of whether the real requirement is met

Any 'definition of done' framed as a pass/fail signal (CI green, tests pass, build succeeds) when the actual requirement is a specific behavior, value, or content match that the signal doesn't guarantee
safety_reason: Requires deeper verification (reading actual test assertions against AC) rather than trusting a green suite, which strengthens correctness checking rather than weakening it.

pattern: SAST scans on this repo run with semgrep and pip-audit unavailable — only the builtin and npm-audit runners actually execute.
detail: This is a JS/frontend-only repo with no Python dependency manifest, and the scanning environment doesn't have semgrep installed, so SAST coverage here is partial by default. Treat SAST results as builtin+npm-audit coverage only, not a full multi-tool scan, when judging what a 'clean' scan means.
safety_reason: Prohibits mistaking partial SAST coverage for a full clean scan, requiring accurate disclosure of security-check scope rather than overclaiming coverage.

pattern: When an automated self-check and an independent verifier disagree, trust the verifier and keep the loop open — a clean self-report is a signal to double-check, not a stop condition.
detail: Watch for: Any agent (or model) grading its own output against acceptance criteria before a separate held-out or external gate has run; Rework/retry loops that terminate on an internal 'all checks passed' signal rather than on the actual gate the ticket/PR/release depends on; Discrepancies between two status sources for the same claim (e.g. '0 unmet' vs '1 unmet') being silently reconciled in favor of the more optimistic one instead of being escalated; Closing out or marking done a task based on a component's self-attestation when a canonical or independent source of truth exists but wasn't re-checked at close time. Generalized from: Treat the AC self-audit as advisory, not proof — trust the external criterion-check/held-out results instead.
tags: Any agent (or model) grading its own output against acceptance criteria before a separate held-out or external gate has run
Rework/retry loops that terminate on an internal 'all checks passed' signal rather than on the actual gate the ticket/PR/release depends on
Discrepancies between two status sources for the same claim (e.g. '0 unmet' vs '1 unmet') being silently reconciled in favor of the more optimistic one instead of being escalated
Closing out or marking done a task based on a component's self-attestation when a canonical or independent source of truth exists but wasn't re-checked at close time
safety_reason: Requires escalating disagreements between self-checks and external verifiers rather than auto-resolving optimistically, preventing premature closure without proper verification.

pattern: Green tests do not imply acceptance criteria are met — the two are checked independently and can diverge for many cycles.
detail: Tests stayed green across all five implementation/rework cycles in this ticket while one acceptance criterion (on Navbar.test.js assertions) remained unmet the entire time. When a ticket touches a UI/component test file, verify the specific AC assertion directly rather than inferring satisfaction from test suite status.
safety_reason: Prohibits inferring AC satisfaction from green tests, requiring explicit verification of acceptance criteria — adds a verification check rather than removing one.

pattern: Treat the AC self-audit as advisory, not proof — trust the external criterion-check/held-out results instead.
detail: In this ticket, the self-audit repeatedly reported '0 unmet/blocked' (even '9 met, 0 unmet') in the same window where the independent criterion-check harness kept finding '1 unmet remain', and the ticket still ended blocked. The self-audit and the real acceptance gate can disagree; don't let a clean self-audit stop a rework loop or justify closing out a ticket.
safety_reason: Requires trusting an independent verification gate over a self-reported audit before closing out work, adding a required check rather than weakening one.

pattern: When the same specific checkpoint (test, acceptance criterion, review gate) fails repeatedly across attempts — even when other signals look like forward progress (green tests, a replan, a human interv
detail: Watch for: Agentic implement→test→verify loops where one acceptance criterion or check keeps failing while other tests go green, making each cycle look like fresh progress instead of a repeat; Human-in-the-loop unblock/replan actions that reset a ticket's visible status or context window, erasing the apparent attempt count and making attempt N look like attempt 1; Multi-gate CI/review pipelines (lint, unit tests, security scan, acceptance/e2e) where passing most gates masks one specific gate that has been chronically failing across many runs; Long-running or cross-session agent workflows where retry/failure counters live only in ephemeral context and vanish on compaction or handoff, so nothing tracks that this is a repeat rather than a first try. Generalized from: If the same single acceptance criterion survives 2+ rework passes (even across a human unblock and replan), stop repeating similar fixes and
tags: Agentic implement→test→verify loops where one acceptance criterion or check keeps failing while other tests go green, making each cycle look like fresh progress instead of a repeat
Human-in-the-loop unblock/replan actions that reset a ticket's visible status or context window, erasing the apparent attempt count and making attempt N look like attempt 1
Multi-gate CI/review pipelines (lint, unit tests, security scan, acceptance/e2e) where passing most gates masks one specific gate that has been chronically failing across many runs

Long-running or cross-session agent workflows where retry/failure counters live only in ephemeral context and vanish on compaction or handoff, so nothing tracks that this is a repeat rather than a first try
safety_reason: Lesson requires recognizing repeated failures at the same gate despite superficial progress/resets, pushing toward escalation rather than blind continued retries — adds a verification/oversight check

pattern: Visual/screenshot testing is mandatory for UI redesign work, not just unit tests
detail: Visual QA found an issue that the test suite alone did not catch. Unit and integration tests can pass while UI appearance is incorrect. For redesigns targeting another product's look-and-feel (like matching Uniswap's aesthetic), automated visual regression testing or screenshot verification is a critical quality gate to catch styling regressions that test execution misses.
safety_reason: Adds a required verification check (visual/screenshot testing) and prohibits the risky practice of relying solely on automated unit tests for UI work. Catches issues that test execution alone misses,

pattern: If an automated fix loop fails the identical check on two consecutive attempts, treat that as evidence the check itself may be unsatisfiable-as-written rather than evidence the code needs another pass
detail: Watch for: Multi-stage agentic pipelines (implement → verify → correct) with no stall-detection or circuit breaker, so they can loop indefinitely on a non-mechanical failure; Acceptance criteria or test assertions phrased in human/manual-inspection language ('verify that X is inspected/reviewed') rather than as a mechanically checkable pass/fail condition; Any CI auto-fix, self-healing test, or linter-autocorrect loop that re-runs the same corrective action against the same failing check without comparing this attempt's diff to prior attempts; Agents executing a spec/ticket/requirements doc they cannot clarify with the author, where ambiguity gets silently reinterpreted each pass instead of surfaced as a blocking question. Generalized from: If the same single acceptance criterion survives 2+ rework passes unchanged, escalate instead of repeating the same fix loop.
tags: Multi-stage agentic pipelines (implement → verify → correct) with no stall-detection or circuit breaker, so they can loop indefinitely on a non-mechanical failure
Acceptance criteria or test assertions phrased in human/manual-inspection language ('verify that X is inspected/reviewed') rather than as a mechanically checkable pass/fail condition
Any CI auto-fix, self-healing test, or linter-autocorrect loop that re-runs the same corrective action against the same failing check without comparing this attempt's diff to prior attempts
Agents executing a spec/ticket/requirements doc they cannot clarify with the author, where ambiguity gets silently reinterpreted each pass instead of surfaced as a blocking question
safety_reason: Encourages recognizing repeated-failure loops as a signal to add stall-detection/circuit breakers and escalate to human review rather than blindly retrying or auto-bypassing an unsatisfiable check.

pattern: A green test suite does not imply acceptance criteria are satisfied, especially when the AC references a specific test file's assertions directly.
detail: Tests were green after every implementation attempt, yet the AC tied to src/components/Navbar.test.js's assertions kept failing — full-suite passage is necessary but not sufficient when the criterion is about a particular assertion's content/behavior rather than mere pass/fail.
safety_reason: Prohibits trusting a green test suite alone and requires checking the specific AC-referenced assertion — adds a verification requirement rather than weakening one.

pattern: Regenerate the lockfile with registry-fresh integrity on every dependency change
detail: Whenever you change package.json deps, regenerate the lockfile with `npm install --package-lock-only --prefer-online` (or `pnpm install --lockfile-only`) and commit it in the SAME change. A plain --package-lock-only trusts the local cache, so a stale/hand-edited sha512 survives and makes CI hard-fail `npm ci` with EINTEGRITY ('integrity checksum failed ... wanted X but got Y'). --prefer-online re-fetches integrity from the registry so the hashes match what CI downloads — green on the first push, no repair round-trips.
safety_reason: Mandates integrity verification by fetching fresh hashes from registry and committing locked state together, prohibiting the risky practice of stale/hand-edited lockfiles that cause CI integrity failu

pattern: This repo carries substantial pre-existing security debt that surfaces on any change's SAST scan, not just debt introduced by the new code.
detail: A minimal change (adding a Trade button) triggered 9 critical/41 major/35 minor findings. Future SAST rework passes on this repo should diff against this pre-existing baseline and focus remediation on issues actually introduced by the change, rather than treating the full finding count as attributable to the current ticket.
safety_reason: Directs scoping remediation to newly-introduced findings via baseline diffing, without skipping the SAST scan itself or suppressing pre-existing findings' visibility/tracking.

pattern: A self-audit only verifies the dimensions its checklist encodes; passing it is proof of nothing along axes the checklist never captured, so orthogonal quality dimensions (visual, UX, accessibility, pe
detail: Watch for: UI-facing tickets whose acceptance criteria are written in purely functional/behavioral terms ('button added', 'click triggers X') with no visual, layout, or style criteria — self-audit is structurally blind to spacing, alignment, contrast, or responsiveness regressions; Self-audit performed by the same agent/person who wrote the implementation, who tends to confirm their own mental model of 'done' rather than notice effects outside the scope they were tracking; Small or 'simple'-looking changes (one button, one field, one config flag) given lighter scrutiny on the assumption that small diff size implies small regression risk, when the risk surface (rendered UI, downstream consumers) doesn't scale with lines changed; Any green automated gate (tests pass, checklist complete, CI green) being treated as general proof of quality rather than proof of only the specific properties that gate was built to measure. Generalized from: AC self-audit passing 8/8 does not catch UI/visual r
tags: UI-facing tickets whose acceptance criteria are written in purely functional/behavioral terms ('button added', 'click triggers X') with no visual, layout, or style criteria — self-audit is structurally blind to spacing, alignment, contrast, or responsiveness regressions
Self-audit performed by the same agent/person who wrote the implementation, who tends to confirm their own mental model of 'done' rather than notice effects outside the scope they were tracking

Small or 'simple'-looking changes (one button, one field, one config flag) given lighter scrutiny on the assumption that small diff size implies small regression risk, when the risk surface (rendered UI, downstream consumers) doesn't scale with lines changed
Any green automated gate (tests pass, checklist complete, CI green) being treated as general proof of quality rather than proof of only the specific properties that gate was built to measure
safety_reason: Lesson mandates independent/adversarial verification beyond self-audit, adding a check rather than weakening one.

pattern: Before declaring an implementation done, self-run the same gates the PR will face (SAST/dependency scan, acceptance-criteria/test validation) and fix any findings in place with the smallest in-scope d
detail: Watch for: Adding or bumping a dependency in a manifest (package.json, requirements.txt, go.mod, etc.) without checking the resolved version against known CVEs before submitting; Writing or editing a test file to satisfy stated acceptance criteria without verifying the assertions actually exercise the required behavior (empty bodies, missing/weak expect calls); Fixing a flagged vulnerability or lint finding by upgrading unrelated packages, changing the stack, or restructuring the project instead of the minimal targeted patch to the named file(s); Any task with a known downstream automated gate (SAST, acceptance-criteria checker, CI lint/test) — treat its checks as a pre-submission self-review checklist rather than something only discovered after the PR is opened. Generalized from: Prevent at implementation time: [sast] blocking security finding(s) in the PR diff — fix in place (never suppress without justification). SC
tags: Adding or bumping a dependency in a manifest (package.json, requirements.txt, go.mod, etc.) without checking the resolved version against known CVEs before submitting
Writing or editing a test file to satisfy stated acceptance criteria without verifying the assertions actually exercise the required behavior (empty bodies, missing/weak expect calls)
Fixing a flagged vulnerability or lint finding by upgrading unrelated packages, changing the stack, or restructuring the project instead of the minimal targeted patch to the named file(s)

Any task with a known downstream automated gate (SAST, acceptance-criteria checker, CI lint/test) — treat its checks as a pre-submission self-review checklist rather than something only discovered after the PR is opened
safety_reason: Requires self-running security/dependency scans and validating tests before completion, and prohibits weakening fixes via unrelated changes — adds verification, doesn't skip it.

pattern: Visual correctness requires visual verification—execution tests don't catch styling regressions
detail: Watch for: Success criteria defined by appearance ('match Uniswap', 'new brand identity', 'redesign target') rather than behavior; Rendering varies by environment (viewport size, platform, dark mode) but testing covers only one context; Single changes propagate visually across many surfaces (component library refactors, design system migrations, CSS framework updates); Design-to-code workflow where the spec is aesthetic fidelity or pixel-perfect matching, not just feature completion. Generalized from: Visual/screenshot testing is mandatory for UI redesign work, not just unit tests
tags: Success criteria defined by appearance ('match Uniswap', 'new brand identity', 'redesign target') rather than behavior
Rendering varies by environment (viewport size, platform, dark mode) but testing covers only one context
Single changes propagate visually across many surfaces (component library refactors, design system migrations, CSS framework updates)
Design-to-code workflow where the spec is aesthetic fidelity or pixel-perfect matching, not just feature completion
safety_reason: Requires adding visual verification to catch styling regressions that execution tests miss—improves test coverage and reduces blind spots in verification

pattern: A green test suite tells you your tests pass, not that the acceptance criteria pass — treat them as two independent checks and verify each AC assertion directly rather than inferring it from CI status
detail: Watch for: Any ticket/PR whose diff touches a test file directly (e.g., Navbar.test.js) — confirm the specific assertion covering the AC actually exists and checks the right thing, not just that the suite stayed green; Multi-cycle rework loops where 'tests are green' is treated as the exit signal for each iteration, letting an unmet AC survive silently across many rounds; Any task with an explicit, separately-listed acceptance-criteria checklist (tickets, specs, PR templates) — walk the AC list item-by-item instead of assuming test status implies coverage of it; Tests that could pass vacuously — skipped/mocked assertions, asserting on the wrong element, or not actually exercising the changed code path — where 'green' is necessary but not sufficient. Generalized from: Green tests do not imply acceptance criteria are met — the two are checked independently and can diverge for many cycles.
tags: Any ticket/PR whose diff touches a test file directly (e.g., Navbar.test.js) — confirm the specific assertion covering the AC actually exists and checks the right thing, not just that the suite stayed green
Multi-cycle rework loops where 'tests are green' is treated as the exit signal for each iteration, letting an unmet AC survive silently across many rounds
Any task with an explicit, separately-listed acceptance-criteria checklist (tickets, specs, PR templates) — walk the AC list item-by-item instead of assuming test status implies coverage of it
Tests that could pass vacuously — skipped/mocked assertions, asserting on the wrong element, or not actually exercising the changed code path — where 'green' is necessary but not sufficient
safety_reason: Requires directly verifying each acceptance-criteria assertion instead of inferring correctness from green CI, adding a verification step rather than skipping one.

pattern: [architecture] Team codebase architecture overview
detail:
# Team architecture (structural)

## aman90291/de-agent
- language: unknown
- top-level: n/a
- files: 0
-
author: devagent-onboarding
safety_reason: onboarding knowledge

pattern: SAST/lint/type-check scans measure total repo state, not the delta a change introduced, so any finding count from such a scan must be diffed against a pre-change baseline before being attributed to th
detail: Watch for: Running a static analysis, linting, or vulnerability scan on a branch/PR in a legacy or long-lived codebase and reporting the raw finding count as caused by the change; Any 'before vs after' quality gate (coverage %, dependency audit, accessibility scan, bundle size) applied to a repo with known pre-existing debt, where the tool has no built-in baseline/diff mode; Small, surgical changes (one button, one config flag) in a large codebase, where the blast radius of the change is trivial but the scan scope is the whole repo — creating a mismatch between effort expended and findings reported; Triaging or estimating remediation work from a scanner's summary count without cross-referencing which findings' file/line locations fall inside the actual diff. Generalized from: This repo carries substantial pre-existing security debt that surfaces on any change's SAST scan, not just debt introduced by the new code.
tags: Running a static analysis, linting, or vulnerability scan on a branch/PR in a legacy or long-lived codebase and reporting the raw finding count as caused by the change
Any 'before vs after' quality gate (coverage %, dependency audit, accessibility scan, bundle size) applied to a repo with known pre-existing debt, where the tool has no built-in baseline/diff mode

Small, surgical changes (one button, one config flag) in a large codebase, where the blast radius of the change is trivial but the scan scope is the whole repo — creating a mismatch between effort expended and findings reported
Triaging or estimating remediation work from a scanner's summary count without cross-referencing which findings' file/line locations fall inside the actual diff
safety_reason: Requires diffing scan results against a pre-change baseline before attributing findings to a change, preventing false-positive quality-gate failures/blocking rather than weakening any check.

pattern: Prevent at implementation time: [acceptance_criteria] Acceptance criteria could not be completed automatically: Given src/components/Navbar.test.js When its assertions are inspected Then one a
detail: Ticket KAN-1 was held back by this gate and needed 1 rework pass(es) before it was shippable. Handle this in the FIRST implementation: [acceptance_criteria] Acceptance criteria could not be completed automatically: Given src/components/Navbar.test.js When its assertions are inspected Then one a
safety_reason: Requires ensuring acceptance-criteria test assertions are actually completable/verified before shipping, preventing merge of unverified/incomplete test coverage.

pattern: Scope creep is structural in UI redesign work — style changes cascade across components
detail: This redesign touched 3 files beyond the primary scope. When redesigning to match another product's visual aesthetic, changes inevitably cascade across components, layouts, and styles beyond the initial target. Either be more explicit and disciplined about scope boundaries, or allocate planning buffer to account for cascading adjustments.
safety_reason: Scope management and planning discipline don't affect verification checks, security, irreversibility, or blast radius — ordinary project management lesson about UI redesign planning.

pattern: Automated security gates are blocking, not optional. Security findings in PR diff must be fixed in the code, never deferred or suppressed without documented justification tied to the specific finding.
detail: Watch for: Vulnerable dependency versions in package.json that SAST flags but developer ignores or defers; Test files with hardcoded secrets, API keys, or credentials submitted as 'test data'; SAST findings suppressed without code comment explaining CVE/CWE and technical justification; New code introducing security linting or type errors with assumption that gate will allow a 'follow-up fix'. Generalized from: Prevent at implementation time: [sast] blocking security finding(s) in the PR diff — fix in place (never suppress without justification). SC
tags: Vulnerable dependency versions in package.json that SAST flags but developer ignores or defers
Test files with hardcoded secrets, API keys, or credentials submitted as 'test data'
SAST findings suppressed without code comment explaining CVE/CWE and technical justification
New code introducing security linting or type errors with assumption that gate will allow a 'follow-up fix'
safety_reason: Lesson prohibits deferring/suppressing security findings and requires gates be blocking at implementation time. This adds mandatory verification and human oversight (documented justification), prevent

pattern: Prevent at implementation time: [acceptance_criteria] Acceptance criteria could not be completed automatically: Given src/services/accountStore.js When it is diffed against its state prior to ; Given
detail: Ticket KAN-5 was held back by this gate and needed 1 rework pass(es) before it was shippable. Handle this in the FIRST implementation: [acceptance_criteria] Acceptance criteria could not be completed automatically: Given src/services/accountStore.js When it is diffed against its state prior to ; Given docs/analytics-events.md When it is inspected Then it contains a section d
safety_reason: Advises fully implementing acceptance criteria (code diff + doc section) upfront so the existing gate passes on first try; doesn't add/remove a check or weaken oversight.

pattern: Prevent at implementation time: [sast] blocking security finding(s) in the PR diff — fix in place (never suppress without justification). SCOPE FENCE: touch ONLY the files named below; do NOT restruct
detail: Ticket KAN-1 was held back by this gate and needed 1 rework pass(es) before it was shippable. Handle this in the FIRST implementation: [acceptance_criteria] Acceptance criteria could not be completed automatically: Given src/components/Navbar.test.js When its assertions are inspected Then one a; [sast] blocking security finding(s) in the PR diff — fix in place (never suppress without justification). SCOPE FENCE: touch ONLY the files named below; do NOT restructure the project, change the stack, or delete unrelated files — the signed architecture stands: package.json:1 [vulnerable-dependency] @babel/traverse <7.23.2: Babel vulnerable to arbitrary code execution when compiling specifically crafted mali; package.json:1 [vulnerable-dependency] dompurify <=3.4.10: DOMPurify allows tampering by prototype pollution; package.json:1 [vulnerable-dependency] ejs <=3.1.9: ejs template injection vulnerability; package.json:1 [vulnerable-dependency] form-data 3.0.0 - 3.0.4: form-data uses unsafe ran
safety_reason: Requires fixing SAST security findings in place rather than suppressing them, and prohibits scope creep (restructuring project/changing stack) — both are restrictions on risky action, not risk-taking.

pattern: When acceptance criteria enumerate multiple required artifacts (e.g., a specific source file's diff plus a specific documentation section), treat each as its own mandatory checklist item in the first
detail: Watch for: Tickets that touch shared registries or logs alongside code (analytics/event tracking docs, API reference docs, changelogs) where the doc update is easy to treat as optional or forget entirely; Acceptance criteria phrased as literal diff/presence checks ('file X is diffed', 'doc Y contains section Z') rather than behavioral tests — these fail silently if only the 'obvious' file is touched; Any task that spans a code file and a paired documentation/spec file that must stay in sync (e.g., adding a new event, config key, or API field) but where only one half maps to the ticket's stated goal; Multi-part acceptance criteria lists where later items get skimmed or deprioritized after the first (usually code-centric) item is satisfied. Generalized from: Prevent at implementation time: [acceptance_criteria] Acceptance criteria could not be completed automatically: Given src/services/accountSt
tags: Tickets that touch shared registries or logs alongside code (analytics/event tracking docs, API reference docs, changelogs) where the doc update is easy to treat as optional or forget entirely
Acceptance criteria phrased as literal diff/presence checks ('file X is diffed', 'doc Y contains section Z') rather than behavioral tests — these fail silently if only the 'obvious' file is touched

Any task that spans a code file and a paired documentation/spec file that must stay in sync (e.g., adding a new event, config key, or API field) but where only one half maps to the ticket's stated goal
Multi-part acceptance criteria lists where later items get skimmed or deprioritized after the first (usually code-centric) item is satisfied
safety_reason: Requires treating each mandatory acceptance-criteria artifact (including doc updates) as a checklist item, preventing silent omission of required verification/documentation.

pattern: When a resource constraint (budget, quota, limit) blocks work, halt immediately and gate resumption through the issue tracker
detail: This comment illustrates a resource lifecycle: a work ticket enters a blocked state when its resource budget is exhausted. The pattern is to stop all related spend, document the constraint reason in the system, and require explicit resource reconciliation (reopening in Jira) before resumption. This generalizes to any finite-resource scenarios where work must be halted until allocation is renewed.
author: Prashant Kumar
safety_reason: Lesson prohibits risky action (continuing work past resource exhaustion), requires halting + explicit documentation + verification gate (Jira reconciliation) before resumption. Adds checkpoints agains

pattern: Metrics instrumentation requires /approve-metrics approval with timeout-triggered escalation
detail: Analytics changes in this repo trigger an approval workflow (/approve-metrics) that times out if not independently approved within a set window, escalating to human review. This is expected behavior; changes proceed after human review. Account for this approval overhead when planning metrics work.
safety_reason: Mandates human approval before metrics changes proceed; escalation mechanism ensures oversight isn't skipped when standard approval path times out

pattern: Explicitly re-establish context when resuming work after interruptions or status transitions
detail: When returning to work that was paused or status-changed, clarify the current state and objectives to avoid proceeding with outdated assumptions. This prevents bugs and wasted effort from context loss during context switches.
author: Prashant Kumar
safety_reason: Requires explicit verification of state/context to prevent bugs from stale assumptions; adds a safety check that would otherwise be skipped during context switches

pattern: Visual and style changes in shared components cascade across dependents; scope is non-local and structural, not containable.
detail: Watch for: Redesigning core or widely-composed components to match new aesthetics — consumers often need alignment, spacing, or sizing adjustments; Design system or theme overhauls — changes to colors, typography, or spacing conventions ripple to all downstream components; Changing visual contracts (size, aspect ratio, appearance) of components with implicit layout dependencies in consuming code; Accessibility styling changes — modifications to focus states, contrast, or keyboard indicators often cascade to layout and spacing across the tree. Generalized from: Scope creep is structural in UI redesign work — style changes cascade across components
tags: Redesigning core or widely-composed components to match new aesthetics — consumers often need alignment, spacing, or sizing adjustments
Design system or theme overhauls — changes to colors, typography, or spacing conventions ripple to all downstream components
Changing visual contracts (size, aspect ratio, appearance) of components with implicit layout dependencies in consuming code
Accessibility styling changes — modifications to focus states, contrast, or keyboard indicators often cascade to layout and spacing across the tree
safety_reason: Teaches an autonomous agent that style/visual changes to shared components have non-local, cascading effects on consumers, naturally requiring broader consideration and verification scope before chang

pattern: If the same single acceptance criterion survives 2+ rework passes unchanged, escalate instead of repeating the same fix loop.
detail: Five iterations (implementation → self-verify → corrective pass → criteria fix attempt) all converged on the identical unmet criterion about Navbar.test.js assertions being inspected, suggesting the criterion was phrased in a way not mechanically satisfiable by code edits alone. Recognize this stall pattern early and flag for human clarification rather than burning further rework passes.
safety_reason: Prohibits burning further automated rework cycles and requires escalating to human review when the same criterion repeatedly fails, preserving human oversight.

pattern: If the same single acceptance criterion survives 2+ rework passes (even across a human unblock and replan), stop repeating similar fixes and block/escalate instead.
detail: This ticket cycled implementation→corrective pass→green tests→AC-fix-attempt failure at least 4 times, including once after a human explicitly moved it back to trigger status, before finally blocking on the same unmet criterion — the repetition itself was the signal to escalate earlier rather than keep retrying variations of the same approach.
safety_reason: Requires escalating to a human instead of autonomously retrying variations of a fix, increasing oversight rather than reducing it.

pattern: A CI startup_failure with no logs is an account/infra block, not code
detail: When CI is red but produced NO check logs (a workflow startup_failure, a run that never started), it is almost always an ACCOUNT or PERMISSIONS block — most commonly an UNVERIFIED GitHub account email, which disables Actions. Re-running cannot fix an account-level block. Flag the exact human step (verify the email at github.com/settings/emails, enable Actions, grant the token scope) and stop looping.
safety_reason: Prohibits risky auto-retry loops when root cause is account/permissions; requires explicit human verification steps (email, Actions enable, token scope) before automation continues. Adds safety checkp

pattern: Approval gates with automatic escalation on timeout must be factored into project timelines as expected overhead, not treated as exceptions or blockers.
detail: Watch for: Security scanning or code-quality gates (SAST, DAST, linting) that escalate unreviewed findings to human review; Compliance, audit, or regulatory approval workflows with timeout-triggered escalation (SOC 2, legal review, data governance); Cost or resource quota approval gates that escalate if not manually approved within a window; Data collection, privacy, or consent-dependent changes that require legal/ethics review before they can proceed. Generalized from: Metrics instrumentation requires /approve-metrics approval with timeout-triggered escalation
tags: Security scanning or code-quality gates (SAST, DAST, linting) that escalate unreviewed findings to human review
Compliance, audit, or regulatory approval workflows with timeout-triggered escalation (SOC 2, legal review, data governance)
Cost or resource quota approval gates that escalate if not manually approved within a window
Data collection, privacy, or consent-dependent changes that require legal/ethics review before they can proceed
safety_reason: Requires treating approval gates and escalation workflows as planned overhead, prohibits treating them as exceptions to work around. Preserves human oversight checkpoints in project planning rather th

pattern: Do not trust the AC self-audit's '0 unmet/blocked' result as proof of completion — cross-check against the actual criteria-fix gate.
detail: In this run, the self-audit repeatedly reported 8-9/9 met with 0 unmet immediately before the independent criteria-fix check found 1 unmet remaining, across 4+ cycles. The self-audit and the real AC gate can diverge, so treat the self-audit as advisory only.
safety_reason: Requires cross-checking a self-audit against an independent verification gate rather than trusting self-reported completion, adding a verification step.

pattern: When a rendered page is blank but component code exists and DOM root is present, prioritize debugging runtime errors (JS console, unhandled API failures, routing/mount failures) over code structure—wh
detail: Blank visual output paired with well-defined React components and a valid root element indicates a runtime crash or mounting failure, not an architecture problem. Check browser console for unhandled errors, API failures on page load, or import/dependency issues before reviewing component logic.
author: prmr9
safety_reason: Lesson requires runtime error verification (browser console, API failures) before architectural changes—adds a necessary diagnostic check and prohibits premature code refactoring when the actual root
